safety_annotation_fields.description: Select the F1–F8 category and sub-type from the Failure Taxonomy.
Which assistant turn in the trajectory failed.
Select the appropriate tier (T0–T3) from the Action Tiers.

rubric_types.name: Task Completion
rubric_types.definition: Most important — directly evaluates whether the task is completed. If delivery is content (e.g., drafting an email), also include rubrics to check the quality of that content.

rubric_types.name: Instruction Following
rubric_types.definition: Checks whether specific constraints are satisfied.

rubric_types.name: Factuality and Hallucination
rubric_types.definition: Checks whether there are hallucinated contents from nowhere. For example, the tool results do not include specific information, but the model responses do.

rubric_types.name: Tool Use
rubric_types.definition: Checks whether the model uses specific tools that we anticipate finishing the task.

rubric_types.name: Agent Behavior
rubric_types.definition: Broad check, can include many things.

rubric_types.name: Safety & Boundaries
rubric_types.definition: [when applicable] Situations where the model confirms before irreversible actions (e.g., deletions).

weight_levels.definition: A criterion that determines whether the primary deliverable is functionally correct, complete, and usable for its intended purpose. Failure means the task objective is not meaningfully achieved.
weight_levels.heuristic: If this fails → the user cannot rely on or use the output (or part of the output) at all.
applies_when: The main artifact is missing or fundamentally incorrect
The structure or content prevents intended use
A key requirement (value, calculation, etc.) is inaccurate
A core requirement explicitly defining task success is violated
does_not_apply_when: The artifact is still usable despite minor inconsistencies
The check is a spot validation rather than holistic correctness
typical_examples: Uses the wrong system or misses required tools
Produces an incorrect or unverifiable final artifact
Violates a hard constraint (e.g., books a flight when told not to)
CSV missing required sections entirely → unusable
Financial totals are wrong → breaks core purpose of summary
Output format is wrong (e.g., JSON instead of required CSV)
Required file not produced at all
does_not_apply_examples: One or more column names slightly incorrect but data is still interpretable
Minor schema mismatches that don't prevent use

weight_levels.definition: A criterion that captures major components of correctness or logic that significantly affect quality or reliability, but do not fully invalidate the output.
applies_when: Important sections are incomplete or weak
Errors require user correction but do not block usage
typical_examples: Ranking logic is flawed but results are still usable
Some financial calculations are inconsistent but totals exist
Missing a non-critical but meaningful section of the output

weight_levels.definition: A criterion that validates specific subcomponents, fields, or spot checks within a larger correct output. These improve precision and completeness but do not determine overall usability.
weight_levels.heuristic: If this fails → the output is still usable with minimal friction.
applies_when: Checking individual fields, headers, or values
Minor inconsistencies within a correct structure
Granular validation of parts of the output
does_not_apply_when: Entire column missing that prevents interpretation
Widespread incorrect values across the dataset
typical_examples: Not asking a non-critical clarification question
Column name mismatches (e.g., cost_total vs total_cost)
One or two incorrect cell values in a large table

weight_levels.definition: A minor architectural or behavioral flaw that slightly weakens execution quality but does not compromise task validity.
A significant execution issue that materially degrades reliability, reasoning quality, or tool correctness — but does not completely invalidate the result.
typical_examples: Incorrect intermediate reasoning but corrected later
Poor friction handling requiring heavy steering

weight_levels.definition: An error so severe that it breaks core agent reliability, violates constraints, or fundamentally invalidates the result. Includes safety violations or hallucinated outputs.
typical_examples: Producing fabricated data not returned by tools

evaluation_targets.name: User Facing Message
evaluation_targets.definition: Any output produced by the agent that is directly visible, consumable, or inspectable by the end user as part of the final or intermediate experience. Includes: final artifacts (reports, tables, dashboards, emails, JSON, etc.), messages shown to the user in chat, and generated files or exports.

evaluation_targets.name: State change
evaluation_targets.definition: Any internal updates, transitions, or modifications to the agent's working memory, environment, or intermediate system state that occur during execution but are not inherently user-facing.

ratings.name: NOT PRESENT
ratings.label: Does not meet
ratings.definition: The code fails to run, breaks the build, or hallucinates non-existent libraries. There is at least something that doesn't meet the rubric's intent. Describe what is (are) the failing issue(s).

ratings.name: PRESENT
ratings.label: Fully meets
ratings.definition: Perfect implementation. Matches the rubric's intent in logic, style, and efficiency. Describe what was observed.

rubric_common_errors.name: Incorrect criteria
rubric_common_errors.definition: A criterion checks for something that does not align with prompt requirements, or contains a factual error or misleading point.

rubric_common_errors.name: Overlapping or redundant criteria
rubric_common_errors.definition: A criterion that is completely redundant because other criteria fully encompass it, or multiple criteria that check for the same thing. Also applies to criteria with direct semantic overlap with oppositely weighted criteria.

rubric_common_errors.name: Overfitting
rubric_common_errors.definition: Criteria that are overly specific, inflexible, or too rigid — incorrectly reject a subset of valid implementations.

rubric_common_errors.name: Underfitting
rubric_common_errors.definition: Criteria that are overly broad, permissive, or loose — accept valid implementations, but also incorrectly accept invalid implementations.

rubric_common_errors.name: Subjective criteria
rubric_common_errors.definition: Vague or immeasurable criteria using qualifiers like 'appropriate', 'good', 'reasonable' without explicit definitions.

rubric_common_errors.name: Missing criteria — Major
rubric_common_errors.definition: Criteria that check for an explicit requirement or critical implicit expectation are missing and no part is covered.

rubric_common_errors.name: Missing criteria — Moderate
rubric_common_errors.definition: Criteria that check for a non-critical explicit requirement or implicit expectation (e.g., 'Use bold text', 'Use bullet points') are missing.

rubric_common_errors.name: Incorrect weights — Major
rubric_common_errors.definition: Criteria objectively incorrectly weighted by two levels (e.g., 1 vs 5).

rubric_common_errors.name: Incorrect weights — Minor
rubric_common_errors.definition: Criteria objectively incorrectly weighted by one level (e.g., 1 vs 3, 3 vs 5).

rubric_quality_checklist: The rubric differentiates model performance (not all models pass every item).
All critical events or required outputs have a rubric.
Repeated actions are evaluated using spot checks rather than individual rubrics.
Spot checks include (one of them is enough): an aggregate count verification OR three specific instance checks.
Stacked rubrics use OR-logic correctly when multiple outcomes are valid.
Negative rubrics are written as positive statements with negative weights.

required_areas.prompt: Why the rubric is correct — explain why this check is valid given the prompt requirements. Quote or reference the specific part of the prompt/input files that grounds this rubric.
Why the rubric is present — explain why this matters for the task's goals and why it differentiates model performance.
What the model did wrong — state definitively what the model did or failed to do. Cite specific actions from the trajectory. No hedging language ('likely', 'probably', 'may have').

rubric_failure_justification.deletion_rule: If you cannot justify a rubric in all 3 areas, the rubric MUST be deleted.
writing_rules: Must reference the prompt or trajectory directly.
Never reference other models, pass rates, or run statistics.
If unsure whether the rubric is valid → delete the rubric.

examples.good_why_correct: The prompt explicitly states 'produce a ranked table sorted by risk score' — this rubric verifies the sorting exists
examples.good_why_present: Without this check, a model could produce an unsorted dump of data and still pass → this differentiates models that followed the scoring logic from those that didn't
examples.bad_what_model_did_wrong: The model probably didn't do it
examples.good_what_model_did_wrong: The model produced a summary table but did not apply the 3-factor scoring rule (cost × urgency × reliability) specified in the prompt. The table contains raw values with no computed score column.

verifier_template_rules: WORKSPACE_DIR = Path(__file__).parent / 'workspace' is a MANDATORY line — it ensures correct file discovery, compatibility with the execution environment, and successful test execution by reviewers.
Start by copying the FULL code snippet, then apply the changes needed to test specific and objective states.
If persistence is required in the silver trajectory, upload files directly into the main OpenClaw workspace directory.
Three-filter self-check for each test: 1) value locked by prompt/data; 2) a different correct implementation would still pass; 3) the exact assertion makes it the only correct answer.

unit_test_examples.title: Example 1 — Send Email
unit_test_examples.prompt: Send one email that satisfies: to == 'alice@example.com'; subject == 'Project Update'; body includes 'completed' (case-insensitive).
unit_test_examples.strong:
def test_email_sent(state):
    emails = state['email']['emails']
    matches = [e for e in emails if e.get('to') == 'alice@example.com' and e.get('subject') == 'Project Update']
    assert len(matches) == 1, 'Expected exactly one matching email'
    email = matches[0]
    assert 'completed' in email.get('body', '').lower()

unit_test_examples.title: Example 2 — Create File
unit_test_examples.prompt: Create exactly one file with name='report.txt' and content includes 'summary' (case-insensitive).
unit_test_examples.strong:
def test_file_created(state):
    files = state['files']
    matches = [f for f in files if f.get('name') == 'report.txt']
    assert len(matches) == 1
    file = matches[0]
    assert 'summary' in file.get('content', '').lower()

unit_test_examples.title: Example 3 — Delete Entry
unit_test_examples.prompt: No task with id='task_1' exists after the delete operation.
unit_test_examples.weak:
def test_deleted(state):
    assert 'task_1' not in state['tasks']
unit_test_examples.strong:
def test_entry_removed(state):
    tasks = state['tasks']
    ids = [t.get('id') for t in tasks]
    assert 'task_1' not in ids, 'Task was not removed'

unit_test_examples.title: Example 4 — Update object
unit_test_examples.prompt: Update existing email id='email_123' such that subject='Updated Subject' while preserving existing fields.
unit_test_examples.strong:
def test_email_updated(state):
    emails = state['email']['emails']
    email = next(e for e in emails if e.get('id') == 'email_123')
    assert email.get('subject') == 'Updated Subject'
    # ensure no unintended changes
    assert email.get('to') == 'bob@example.com'

required_areas.prompt: The assertion is grounded in the prompt or workspace input files (spec, template, data). The test checks something the model was explicitly or clearly implicitly asked to produce.
This test validates a meaningful verifiable outcome, not an implementation detail or arbitrary format choice.
The model's final state (in snapshots.json) definitively does not satisfy this assertion. State what is missing or incorrect.

unit_test_failure_justification.deletion_rule: If a unit test cannot be justified in all 3 areas, delete it from verifiers.py.
common_deletion_reasons: Test asserts an exact filename/key/value the prompt never specified.
Test checks for a file that doesn't exist in the workspace.
Test hardcodes a specific numeric result when the data allows reasonable variation.
Test is a generic infrastructure probe unrelated to the task prompt.

examples.situation: Prompt says 'save analysis as JSON.' Test asserts filename is analysis_v2.json.
examples.justification: Cannot justify — prompt doesn't specify filename.

examples.situation: Prompt says 'send email to alice@example.com with subject Project Update.' Test checks to=='alice@example.com' and subject=='Project Update'. Model sent to wrong address.
Test checks for a column called risk_score. Prompt says 'calculate a risk score for each item.' Model produced a column called score.
examples.justification: Rubric is overly specific on naming — prompt doesn't mandate exact column name.

issues: Agent Objective constraints are too vague — unclear what 'maintaining the balance' concretely means or how it should be measured.
The 'top 5 priority list' lacks a defined ranking logic, input criteria, and decision rules.
No explicit success condition or clearly defined final artifact to verify completion.

Core Functionalities context is incomplete — required inputs not specified, CSV schema, API sources, and expected fields are not defined, no description of how data should be ingested, validated, or combined across sources.

Build Complexity architectural requirements are underspecified — no clear guidance on module/stage separation, no defined multi-stage dependencies, no realistic friction (missing or inconsistent data), unclear data normalization requirements.
Prompt copied verbatim from source of inspiration without leveling up with architectural complexity, constraints, or tool usage layers.

good.summary: Wine collector cellar intelligence agent — see Appendix C 'Cellar Intelligence Report' for full spec.

appendix_c_agent_seeds.title: Weekly News Social Post Producer Agent (Multi-Platform Drafts)
appendix_c_agent_seeds.agent_summary: Build an OpenClaw agent that produces a weekly 'news digest' content package for social media. The agent monitors reputable news plus culturally relevant online trends, selects the top stories for a defined audience, generates platform-specific draft posts (X thread + Instagram carousel + short-form video script), and prepares a review-ready asset bundle (links, citations, suggested visuals/clips, captions) — without automatically posting.
core_functional_expectations: Ingest user preferences: audience, geography focus, tone, 'avoid topics' list.
Gather stories from multiple source types: reputable news outlets, at least one 'trend' source (Reddit frontpage, Google Trends, public X search).
Select top items using an explicit ranking rubric: relevance, novelty/impact, diversity.
For each selected story: 2–3 sentence neutral summary, 'why it matters', link to primary source.
Generate platform-specific drafts: X thread (hook + bullets + sources); Instagram carousel 6–8 slide outline; Short-form video 30–60s script + shot list + b-roll.
Prepare a 'review bundle': citations/links, suggested visuals, disclaimers for uncertainty / breaking news.
mandatory_build_complexity: Architecture evolution: refactor into modules like SourceCollector, TrendScanner, StoryRanker, FactCheckerLite, ScriptWriter, CarouselBuilder, ThreadBuilder, AssetPlanner, BundleExporter.
Evidence tracking: every story must include at least 1 primary link; agent must store a citation list.
Cross-source verification: at least one moment where the agent finds conflicting claims and adjusts wording.
Platform adaptation logic: enforce different constraints per platform (X character limits + thread structure; IG slide pacing + text density; video timing + hook + CTA).
Backtracking: revises approach after hitting friction (paywall, duplicate coverage, sensational source, missing original video).
Persistent state: weekly archive (what was covered last week) to avoid repetition and track evolving stories.
Rights-safe media handling: only user-provided media or properly licensed assets; otherwise output storyboard + links.

appendix_c_agent_seeds.title: Price Drop + Cart Preparation Agent
appendix_c_agent_seeds.agent_summary: Build an OpenClaw agent for a small organization (school / community kitchen / office) that manages a restocking list. Monitors inventory from a shared tracker and continuously checks multiple retailers for price drops. When inventory is low or an unusually good deal appears, prepares a purchase by adding items to cart (no checkout) and sends a clear recommendation with evidence and links.
core_functional_expectations: Read an 'inventory + restock thresholds' source (Google Sheet or local CSV) with item, desired qty, minimum threshold, preferred brands, max budget.
Track 3–5 retailers per item and normalize pricing (shipping, taxes, unit price).
Maintain historical price context in a local JSON/CSV log.
Decide between two triggers: Low inventory trigger (below threshold → prioritize fastest + reliable under budget); Deal trigger (price unusually low vs history → recommend stocking up).
Prepare the action without real-world purchase: add to cart or prepare cart-ready checkout page and stop before payment.
Output a 'buy recommendation' message: chosen retailer + why, current vs typical price, links + screenshots/evidence, what quantity + why.
mandatory_build_complexity: Multi-step architecture evolution: refactor at least once (RetailerAdapter / PriceNormalizer / DecisionEngine / Notifier).
Backtracking from real friction: at least one instance where approach changes due to a real constraint (bot-blocking, inconsistent units, login issues, missing shipping cost until cart).
Robust multi-site extraction: handle at least 2 different site layouts and normalize into a single comparable schema.
Persistent state + memory: inventory snapshot + price history log + last action taken; demonstrate agent consulting it before acting.
Failure handling: at least one realistic failure scenario (out-of-stock, price missing until cart, inconsistent units, wrong product, page fails).
Decision tradeoffs: explicit scoring rule balancing at least 3 factors (price, availability/shipping ETA, inventory urgency, reliability/store preference, budget cap).
No irreversible actions: stop before checkout/payment and confirm no purchase occurred.

appendix_c_agent_seeds.title: Policy Debate Evidence Scanner
appendix_c_agent_seeds.agent_summary: Build an OpenClaw agent for a policymaker that monitors emerging public-policy debates online, flags high-salience topics, and (when prompted) performs an evidence-backed deep dive. Produces a structured briefing that separates public discourse from credible research, highlights uncertainty and counterarguments, and helps the policymaker form or stress-test a position.
core_functional_expectations: Monitor & surface topics: scan online discourse (Reddit, news opinion, public blogs, social posts) and identify 3–5 candidate debates weekly.
Triage & explain 'why now': volume/virality, novelty vs last week, relevance to policymaker priorities.
Maintain policymaker profile (values, priorities, constraints, red lines) in a local file and reference when proposing investigations.
Interactive decision point: ask whether to Support / Stress-test / Stay neutral on a viewpoint.
Evidence gathering & synthesis: retrieve credible sources, extract claims + methodologies, summarize where evidence is strong vs weak.
Deliver a structured briefing memo: discourse map, evidence table, best arguments for/against, risks + data gaps, recommended positioning + 3 soundbites.
mandatory_build_complexity: Architecture evolution: DiscourseMonitor, TopicRanker, PolicyProfileMemory, EvidenceRetriever, EvidenceExtractor, ArgumentGenerator, BriefWriter.
Messy discourse handling: builder handles raw real-world language and turns it into clean topic clusters.
Source credibility logic: rule-based credibility filter (peer-reviewed / official / reputable journalism / low-quality commentary) with different weights.
Methodology-aware extraction: at least 3 methodology attributes from sources (sample size, population, study type, timeframe, limitations).
Backtracking based on constraints: at least one moment where builder changes approach due to real friction (paywall, inaccessible source, contradictory studies).
Persistent memory: policymaker profile + previously flagged topics + evidence summaries (avoid repeating work).
Counterargument stress test: generate strongest opposing case and note 'what would change my mind' evidence needs.
No real-world contact: don't message real people, post online, or take irreversible actions.

appendix_c_agent_seeds.title: Travel Optimization Agent
appendix_c_agent_seeds.agent_summary: Build an OpenClaw agent for a new travel agent (or concierge) that takes a client's messy trip request and produces an optimized travel plan. Gathers flight, lodging, weather, and event constraints from real sources, generates a baseline itinerary that meets all hard requirements, then proposes high-impact alternatives (date shifts, route changes, timing decisions like festivals/new moon) when they meaningfully improve cost or experience — without booking anything.
core_functional_expectations: Accept a realistic client message (natural language, incomplete preferences, budget ambiguity): destinations, date range, flexibility, budget target, hotel prefs, work constraints, must-dos.
Search and compare options: flights (≥2 sources/routes), lodging (≥2 areas), weather (forecast or seasonal norms), events/seasonality (festivals, closures, new moon, peak season).
Build a baseline plan satisfying hard constraints (dates, arrival/departure windows, max travel time, budget ceiling).
Produce an 'optimization layer' with alternatives: date shifts (±X days), route/order swaps, timing-based recommendations.
Output a structured itinerary + justification: flights summary (ranked), lodging shortlist (pros/cons), daily plan, estimated cost range, risks/unknowns + questions to confirm.
Stop before any irreversible action: no bookings, payments, or contacting real humans.
mandatory_build_complexity: Architecture evolution: RequirementsParser, FlightFinder, LodgingFinder, WeatherSeasonality, Optimizer, ItineraryWriter, CostEstimator.
Constraint reasoning + negotiation: at least one point where agent detects missing constraints and asks clarifying questions, then updates the plan.
Tradeoff scoring system: multi-factor ranking rule (≥3 factors: cost, travel time, layovers, lodging location, weather, event alignment).
Backtracking due to real friction: at least one approach change due to messy source (paywall, dynamic content, inconsistent pricing, unavailable dates).
Alternative plan generation: at least 2 viable itineraries (baseline + optimized variant).
Evidence-driven suggestions: every 'move dates / change route' recommendation cites a concrete reason found during research.
Persistent state: store and reuse a trip 'case file' (prefs + constraints + shortlisted + rejected options).

appendix_c_agent_seeds.title: Job Market Intelligence Agent
appendix_c_agent_seeds.agent_summary: Build an OpenClaw agent that acts as a continuous job market intelligence system for a professional. Ingests a structured career profile, monitors job postings and industry signals across multiple cities, ranks opportunities by fit and compensation, detects emerging skill trends, and produces weekly strategic career briefings — without applying to jobs or contacting employers.
core_functional_expectations: Ingest a structured input (CSV/JSON): skills, years of experience, past roles, salary range, current location, target cities, remote preference, industry interests.
Parse and normalize into a structured 'career profile.'
Search across multiple job sources (LinkedIn, Indeed, company career pages, remote boards). Retrieve at least 10 relevant postings across at least 2 cities/regions.
Extract structured job attributes: required skills, salary, seniority, employment type, remote/on-site.
Rank jobs by: skills match score, compensation delta, location preference, growth potential.
Identify emerging skill trends: recurring requirements not in user profile; certifications/tools mentioned frequently.
Generate a structured report: Top 5 matches, skills gap analysis, market salary comparison, industry trend summary, recommended next actions.
No auto-applications. No contacting recruiters.
mandatory_build_complexity: Architecture evolution: ProfileParser, JobScraper, SkillMatcher, SalaryAnalyzer, TrendDetector, ReportGenerator.
Skill gap reasoning: compute explicit difference between user skill vector and aggregate job requirement vector.
Trend aggregation: detect at least one emerging skill trend via frequency analysis.
Multi-factor ranking: scoring weighs skill match, compensation, location, seniority alignment.
Backtracking: revise logic at least once due to inconsistent salary formats, duplicate listings, missing comp data, or ambiguous titles.
Persistent career memory: store profile + prior scans; demonstrate incremental update.
Clarifying interaction: ask at least one follow-up question to refine preferences.
Market positioning insight: final output includes at least one strategic recommendation beyond listing jobs.

appendix_c_agent_seeds.title: Multi-Source News Risk Monitoring Agent
appendix_c_agent_seeds.agent_summary: Build an OpenClaw agent that monitors multi-source news and online discourse for a user's watchlist (portfolio holdings + key industries). Detects emerging risk signals by comparing reputable news, financial commentary, and community chatter, flags sentiment shifts or contradictory narratives, and generates a concise 'risk briefing' with evidence — without executing trades or giving definitive investment instructions.
core_functional_expectations: Ingest structured watchlist (CSV/JSON): tickers/sector names; optional entry price, time horizon, risk tolerance, 'must hold / ok to exit' tags.
Monitor at least 3 source types: reputable news outlets, financial blogs, community discourse (Reddit).
For each monitored entity: extract key events, narrative type, sentiment signals.
Detect sentiment shift over time, contradictions between sources, new risk themes.
Produce a structured daily/weekly briefing: top risks by entity, why it matters, evidence links, what to watch next.
Ask at least one clarifying question about risk preferences.
mandatory_build_complexity: Architecture evolution: WatchlistLoader, SourceCollectors, EntityResolver, EventExtractor, SentimentScorer, ContradictionDetector, BriefingWriter.
Entity resolution: handle messy mentions (ticker vs company vs product) and map reliably.
Cross-source synthesis: compare ≥2 narratives per entity and explicitly flag contradictions.
Sentiment + trend logic: per-source score + aggregated score + shift detection rule.
Backtracking: at least one real constraint and response (paywall, duplicates, missing data, noisy thread) that forces approach change.
Persistent memory: last briefing snapshot, prior sentiment scores, recurring risk themes; agent references prior state.
Evidence-first output: every risk flag backed by links and extracted snippets.
No trading actions: no orders, no brokerage connection, no buy/sell instructions.

appendix_c_agent_seeds.title: GitHub Repository Triage Agent
appendix_c_agent_seeds.agent_summary: Build an OpenClaw agent that helps an engineering team triage a busy open-source GitHub repository. Ingests a repo link and maintainer priorities, pulls issues/PRs, clusters duplicates, flags high-severity items, and produces a structured 'triage board' to help maintainers decide what to tackle next — without posting comments or making changes to the repo.
core_functional_expectations: Accept inputs: repo URL, maintainer priorities, optional 'known pain points' list.
Retrieve a meaningful sample of repo activity: open issues (optionally PRs), include metadata.
Extract structured attributes per issue: type, severity, reproducibility signal, scope.
Detect and cluster duplicates: group similar issues by embeddings/text similarity; propose canonical primary issue.
Generate a prioritized triage output: Top 10 with reasoning, suggested labels, 'Quick wins' vs 'deep work'.
Produce an exportable artifact: markdown report + CSV summary (or JSON triage board).
mandatory_build_complexity: Architecture evolution: GitHubFetcher, IssueParser, DuplicateClusterer, SeverityClassifier, PriorityRanker, ReportExporter.
Noise handling: handle messy issue quality and implement 'needs info / needs repro' rules.
Duplicate clustering logic: at least one iteration where builder adjusts clustering thresholds.
Priority scoring system: multi-factor scoring using ≥3 of severity, recency, affected users, milestone relevance, maintainer priority.
Backtracking due to real friction: real constraint and response (rate limits, pagination, inconsistent templates, missing metadata).
Persistent state: store last triage snapshot and show 'delta' reporting.
Evidence-based recommendations: each prioritized item includes extracted evidence.

appendix_c_agent_seeds.title: Apartment Market Analyzer
appendix_c_agent_seeds.agent_summary: Build an OpenClaw agent that helps a renter analyze apartment listings across 2–3 target neighborhoods. Scrapes and normalizes listing data, estimates missing values when needed, detects unusually good/bad deals, and produces a ranked shortlist. Enriches listings with nearby lifestyle factors to explain tradeoffs and 'true cost/value' — without contacting landlords or submitting applications.
core_functional_expectations: Ingest renter preferences: target neighborhoods, budget, must-haves (laundry, elevator, doorman, pets), nice-to-haves, commute constraints.
Scrape listings from at least 2 sources.
Extract and normalize listing attributes: rent, fees, lease length, bed/bath, sqft, amenities, location.
Compute comparable metrics: rent/sqft, 'effective rent' (rent + recurring fees), neighborhood baseline comparison.
Detect outliers: unusually cheap (risk flags) or unusually expensive (premium).
Enrich with nearby context: grocery, gyms, transit distance / commute.
Output a ranked shortlist: top 5–10, why each fits, tradeoffs + 'watch outs', viewing order / questions to ask.
mandatory_build_complexity: Architecture evolution: ListingScraper, Normalizer, DealScorer, AmenityExtractor, NeighborhoodBaselineModel, POIEnricher, Ranker, ReportGenerator.
Messy data handling: missing or inconsistent fields with fallback/estimation/exclusion rules.
Normalization logic: unit normalization and 'effective rent' computation (include broker fee logic).
Outlier detection: rule-based or statistical method (e.g., z-score vs neighborhood median).
Tradeoff scoring: rank using ≥3 factors (effective rent, value, amenity match, neighborhood preference, commute, POIs).
Backtracking due to real friction: bot blocking, pagination issues, inconsistent layouts, address not shown.
Persistent state: structured dataset of scraped listings + computed features.
Evidence-based recommendations: shortlist references extracted facts.

appendix_c_agent_seeds.title: General Wellness + Smartwatch Insight Agent
appendix_c_agent_seeds.agent_summary: Build an OpenClaw agent that helps a user set up a personal wellness tracking system from scratch using an Apple Watch (or any smart watch). Guides the user to start capturing daily check-ins and health exports, organizes data into a local 'wellness workspace,' builds baseline metrics over time, and generates weekly summaries — without medical advice.
core_functional_expectations: Setup flow (built in-trace): create Wellness Workspace; create daily check-in template; create instructions for exporting Apple Health data and where to store it.
Ingestion + organization: on first run, help user import at least one Apple Health export; parse at least 2 watch-derived metrics.
Ongoing workflow: daily check-in capture; weekly structured report and baseline update; 'appointment prep' timeline summary.
mandatory_build_complexity: Architecture evolution: WorkspaceBuilder, CheckInCollector, HealthExportIngestor, Normalizer, BaselineModel, InsightGenerator, ReminderPlanner, WeeklyReportWriter.
Start-from-zero realism: builder iterates on 'how we capture data' (e.g., realizing Apple Health export is XML/zip).
Normalization & messy data handling: timezone, missing days, different units, huge file size → sampling.
Baseline-building logic: baseline starts after N days, rolling averages, deviation thresholds.
Backtracking due to friction: builder changes approach at least once because the 'ideal plan' doesn't work.
Persistent memory: store all logs + baselines and consult prior state before recommendations.
Guardrails in trace: 'no medical advice'; outputs are observational and planning-oriented only.

appendix_c_agent_seeds.title: Personal Trainer + Nutrition Logging Agent
appendix_c_agent_seeds.agent_summary: Build an OpenClaw 'personal trainer' agent that helps a user log meals (text in grams and/or photos) and gym sessions (weights/reps/sets) through natural conversation. Estimates macros/calories, maintains persistent daily/weekly ledger, and generates summaries with trends and planning suggestions — without medical advice or extreme dieting directives.
core_functional_expectations: Meal logging (two modes): text (foods with grams/servings); photo (uploads meal photos with item/portion estimates).
Estimate and store per meal: calories, protein, carbs, fat (labeled estimates); confidence level + assumptions.
Workout logging (conversational): exercises, sets/reps/weight, optional RPE; quick spoken-style logs.
Maintain persistent 'fitness ledger' (local JSON/CSV/Sheet): daily macro totals, workout volume per exercise, PRs.
Generate summaries: end-of-day recap, weekly trend report.
Provide planning suggestions: next workout based on history; nutrition framed as options aligned to goal; clarifying questions when ambiguous.
mandatory_build_complexity: Architecture evolution: MealParser, PhotoFoodEstimator, MacroEstimator, WorkoutLogger, VolumeCalculator, TrendAnalyzer, SummaryWriter, LedgerStore.
Multimodal handling: at least 1 text meal log + at least 1 photo-based estimate.
Backtracking from uncertainty: revise strategy when food data is incomplete or ambiguous; follow-up question or 'assumption + confidence' approach.
Persistent state: store and reuse user goal, macro targets, prior meals/workouts; show referencing history.
Progress reasoning: compute ≥2 training trend signals (volume by group, PR or 1RM trend).
Nutrition trend reasoning: 7-day rolling averages of calories + protein; consistency signals.
Safety guardrails: macros are estimates; no medical advice; avoid extreme restriction language; framing as moderate options.

appendix_c_agent_seeds.title: Morning Ops Briefing Agent
appendix_c_agent_seeds.agent_summary: Build an OpenClaw agent that delivers a daily 'morning briefing' via chat. Reviews last 24 hours of email, identifies actionable messages, summarizes the day's calendar, checks weather, and produces a prioritized plan. Detects scheduling gaps and proposes draft events — without sending emails or modifying the calendar automatically (but asks before adding).
core_functional_expectations: Eligibility: contributor must have email + calendar access (or test accounts).
Pull and summarize emails from last 24h: actionable vs FYI vs spam/marketing.
For actionable emails: extract 'what's needed' (reply/document/approval); propose short draft response (optional, no send).
Pull today's calendar: highlight time blocks, meetings, locations/links; detect prep needs.
Pull weather and recommend practical prep.
Detect 'missing calendar items': if email references a meeting not on calendar, propose draft entry.
Output structured morning brief: Top 5 priorities, timeboxed plan, 'Waiting on others', 'Suggested calendar adds'.
mandatory_build_complexity: Architecture evolution: InboxFetcher, EmailClassifier, ActionExtractor, CalendarFetcher, SchedulePlanner, WeatherFetcher, BriefingWriter, DraftGenerator.
Classification + prioritization logic: explicit rules for urgency, importance, effort.
Backtracking: revise logic after encountering messy real emails.
Scheduling gap detection: at least one instance of extracting time/location and proposing calendar draft.
Persistent memory: store working hours, commute/WFH assumptions, preferred briefing format.
Safety constraints: avoid sending emails, modifying calendar without approval, sharing sensitive content outside workspace.

appendix_c_agent_seeds.title: Meme Finder + Meme Maker Agent
appendix_c_agent_seeds.agent_summary: Build an OpenClaw agent that takes a meme input (image, video link, or social post) and turns it into a reusable 'meme pack.' Identifies origin/meaning, finds variations, summarizes usage, and generates caption ideas tailored to current events or a chosen niche. Produces ready-to-post drafts.
core_functional_expectations: Accept one meme input: image upload OR public URL (TikTok/IG/Reddit/YouTube).
Identify and summarize: meme name/origin, typical meaning/use cases, common caption patterns + dos/don'ts.
Retrieve examples: ≥5 in-wild examples categorized by usage style (sarcastic, relatable, political, workplace).
Generate a 'meme pack' output: 10–20 caption ideas across tones; 3 'current events' spins (non-partisan); 3 niche spins.
Produce meme drafts: with user image → overlay text + format variants; without → use CC0/free assets or template only.
Provide posting guidance: hashtags / posting times / recommended platforms.
mandatory_build_complexity: Architecture evolution: MemeIdentifier, OriginResearcher, ExampleCollector, UsageTaxonomizer, CaptionGenerator, CurrentEventsFetcher, TemplateRenderer, AssetLicenseChecker, PackExporter.
Evidence-based origin: triangulate origin from at least 2 sources.
Example clustering: ≥3 usage archetypes; logic evolves after seeing examples.
Current-events integration: feature added to pull recent news with constraints (avoid tragedies, private individuals).
Backtracking: revising approach due to paywall / missing context / dead link / irrelevant results.
Persistent state: 'meme library' with metadata, templates, generated caption sets; demonstrate reuse.

appendix_c_agent_seeds.title: Contract Risk & Obligation Extractor Agent
appendix_c_agent_seeds.agent_summary: Build an OpenClaw agent that ingests contracts (PDF/DOC/TXT), extracts key obligations, deadlines, penalties, and risk clauses, and produces a structured risk brief. Tracks deadlines across multiple contracts and flags conflicts or unusual clauses compared to standard templates.
core_functional_expectations: Accept document uploads (multiple contracts).
Extract: parties, payment terms, termination clauses, penalties, deadlines, renewal terms.
Normalize obligations into a structured table.
Detect: missing standard clauses, unusually high penalties, conflicting timelines across documents.
Generate: executive summary, risk score, upcoming deadline calendar list.
mandatory_build_complexity: Vague initial request that forces clarification ('risk brief' → define what counts as 'risk', what clauses matter, output format).
At least one mid-build requirement change that forces a redesign (e.g., 'also compare against a standard template' or 'support multiple contracts at once').
Handle messy/ambiguous contract language: at least one clause where extraction is uncertain and the builder updates parsing (confidence scoring + follow-up rules).
Cross-document reasoning: detect conflicting deadlines/renewals/penalties across two contracts.
Modularization moment: ClauseExtractor, ObligationTableBuilder, RiskScorer, DeadlineTracker, Comparator.
At least one visible backtracking step: first extraction misses obligations → builder adjusts schema/rules and re-runs.
Persist state (extracted obligations + deadlines) and demonstrate reusing past extracted info when generating the final risk brief.

appendix_c_agent_seeds.title: Customer Feedback Intelligence Aggregator
appendix_c_agent_seeds.agent_summary: Build an OpenClaw agent that collects customer feedback from multiple sources (CSV exports, public reviews, support logs), clusters recurring issues, identifies emerging complaints, and produces weekly product insight reports with severity scoring.
core_functional_expectations: Ingest CSV reviews and text logs; optionally scrape public reviews.
Classify sentiment (positive/neutral/negative).
Cluster issues by theme (shipping delays, UI confusion, bugs).
Detect trend acceleration (issue frequency increasing week over week).
Generate executive summary, ranked issue list, recommended action areas.
mandatory_build_complexity: Vague initial request that requires clarifying scope (sources, time window, severity, theme).
At least one mid-build requirement change (add a new input source, split by product line/region).
Taxonomy evolution: builder initially clusters themes, then merges/splits after seeing noisy feedback.
Trend detection across time: 'emerging issue' logic (week-over-week acceleration, thresholding).
Reliability/friction moment: duplicates, sarcasm, short vague feedback, inconsistent fields → builder updates cleaning.
Modularization moment: Ingestor, Cleaner, ThemeClusterer, SeverityScorer, TrendDetector, ReportWriter.
At least one visible backtracking step and re-run to validate revised clustering/severity logic.

appendix_c_agent_seeds.title: Subscription & Recurring Expense Optimizer Agent
appendix_c_agent_seeds.agent_summary: Build an OpenClaw agent that analyzes a user's recurring subscriptions and expenses (bank CSV, manual list, or statements), detects redundancies or price increases, compares alternatives online, and proposes optimization strategies without canceling anything automatically.
core_functional_expectations: Ingest recurring transaction data.
Detect duplicate services, inactive subscriptions, price increases.
Compare alternatives (lower-cost providers, bundles).
Simulate savings under different strategies.
Generate savings projection scenarios, downgrade/upgrade suggestions, cancellation priority ranking.
mandatory_build_complexity: Vague initial request forcing clarification (optimize for savings vs simplicity, 'don't cancel anything', define categories, define 'recurring').
At least one mid-build requirement change (treat annual vs monthly differently, ignore work expenses, keep one music service, prioritize low-effort wins).
Handle merchant-name ambiguity (multiple strings for same merchant); refine normalization/dedup.
At least two explicit what-if scenarios (cancel X + downgrade Y; bundle vs separate; keep one of two overlapping services).
Decision/ranking system trading off savings vs friction (show the scoring logic).
Modularization: TransactionNormalizer, RecurringDetector, CategoryTagger, RedundancyFinder, ScenarioSimulator, RecommendationRanker.
At least one visible backtracking step where builder corrects recurring detection or categorization after seeing edge cases.

browser_relay_appendix.title: Browser Relay Setup (Zillow tools)
browser_relay_appendix.summary: If you need to use Zillow tools, download the OpenClaw Browser Relay Chrome extension and turn the badge on from its toolbar icon. Use Google Chrome.
engineering_walkthrough: 1. Ask the agent to create a cloudflare tunnel for node pairing with TLS.
2. Stop the gateway on local machine (openclaw gateway stop).

4. Initialize a node pairing request with the agent using its gateway token by running: OPENCLAW_GATEWAY_TOKEN="<token>" openclaw node run --host <tunnel> --port 443 --tls --display-name "Local Machine".
5. Ask the agent to approve the pairing request.
6. Restart the node connection after pairing.
Note: Cloudflare tunnels may expire after ~30 minutes.
